text: Hello, this is Adam Rothermel, Little City Big Town Surgery.
I'm presenting here a forty-nine year old male who presented for a routine screening colonoscopy and was found to have this very large seven centimeter nearly obstructing sigmoid colon tumor.
Of course, biopsy proved he had no carcinoma, found by our excellent gastroenterologist.
Metastatic evaluation was negative for any evidence of distant disease. CEA was normal.
And so we proceeded with minimally invasive sigmoid colectomy.

I'd just like to say I've benefited so tremendously from so many of you posting your videos, showing your technique, and I just can't tell you how much I've appreciated that and learned from that and how much it's benefited my patients. And so I thought I needed to try to pay it forward and I am doing my best to learn how to edit these videos and narrate, and hopefully somebody can get something from this. But if not, hopefully I can get something from it by receiving your feedback about the operations.
The surgeon begins a lateral to medial dissection along the white line of Toldt, medializing the left colon and progressing towards the left side of the rectal mesentery.

I like to identify the ureter before proceeding to the medial to lateral. And I find that I can be more confident in that medial to lateral dissection. And I can identify the ureter more readily if I've already seen it from lateral.
So I generally take the time to do a good lateral to medial mobilization and then flip it over and work on the medial to lateral as you'll see here.
The surgeon flips the sigmoid colon over, with the assistant holding it out of the pelvis, and elevates the rectal mesentery.

Really learned a great deal from Dr. Mark Solomon, Dr. Daniel Popowich here on elevating that rectal mesentery and finding the right plane. We'll begin that medial to lateral approach and work our way up to the IMA pedicle.
Clearly I'm using all four of the robotic arms. At this point I have
The robotic setup includes one right arm and two left arms, with the camera positioned to the right and slightly above the umbilicus on the patient.
We've been working with the X-platform for several years. More recently though, we were able to have the benefit of two new XI robots, which has really been wonderful.
So we have a good number of surgeons at our institution that do robotics. Most heavily general surgery, but we do have urology and OBGYN as well.
The surgeon uses robotic arms to dissect the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) proximal to the takeoff of the superior rectal and left colic arteries, revealing the IMA pedicle.
The vessel sealer is used to ligate the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) pedicle effectively. The surgeon then proceeds to dissect further to isolate the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV).
to make sure I kind of release tension on that before actually taking the vessel so it doesn't retract which always makes me a little bit nervous

The surgeon continues the medial to lateral dissection, separating the left colic mesentery from the retroperitoneum, progressing towards the splenic flexure to gain length on the descending colon for the anastomosis.

The surgeon encounters atypical adhesions between the colonica papilloica and omentum at the splenic flexure, carefully taking time to separate these structures to ensure adequate length for the procedure. The lateral mobilization is completed up to the splenic flexure.

The surgeon identifies the proximal resection margin, located at least ten centimeters above the tumor. Using a vessel sealer extend, they begin dissecting through the mesentery starting just above the previously ligated IMA and IMV, ensuring the proximal resection margin is completely cleared.

The surgeon performs specimen extraction through a Pfannenstiel incision, approximately five centimeters in length, due to the large tumor size. The per-string device is used, and the anvil is placed back in position.

I am doing natural orifice, you know, trans-anal specimen removal for a lot of the diverticulitis patients, which has been really nice for those patients to avoid that fan and steel incision, which doesn't seem to hurt people too much.
The surgeon clears the rectum at the distal resection margin, following the use of a stapler through a twelve millimeter port site.

The surgeon uses indocyanine green (ICG) to assess perfusion at the distal resection margin, then adjusts the camera to evaluate the proximal resection margin, confirming adequate blood flow in both areas.
The surgeon uses a stapler with a blue load to transect the rectum at the distal resection margin.

The surgeon checks the reach of the anvil, which appears satisfactory, before undocking the robotic system. A 29mm EEA stapler is used, with the assistant holding the anvil at the bed. The surgeon completes the anastomosis from below.

So again, I just want to say thank you so much to all of you that have posted your videos that I've learned so much from. I do this and post this case in the hopes of continuing to learn from you and gain from your experience, and maybe I have some experience to offer as well. So thank you all so much, and I hope you enjoyed.
